entries.what_they_read: I saw 'Trade player tokens that move with real stats' up top, then the three-step breakdown — get fake money, find a mispriced player, buy or sell the gap. The plain-English explainer box called it 'a pretend stock market for NBA players.'
entries.their_interpretation: So it's a sandbox — they're modeling NBA players like equities, price driven by actual box scores, and you're hunting the spread between what the stats say something's worth and what the crowd is paying. That's a value-investing loop dressed in basketball clothes.
entries.their_decision: I'd click 'Claim $100' and see what the actual player cards look like.
entries.their_rationale: No account, no seed phrase, no real money — the friction cost is basically zero and the downside is nothing, so the only question is whether the underlying mechanic is interesting enough to poke at. The fair-value versus market-price framing is the thing I want to stress-test.
confusion_notes: 'AWAITING TIP-OFF' with 'Prices move after tonight's games' — I don't know if this is a live feed or just flavor text sitting there permanently
What exactly is the 'fair-value price' formula? Stats-based is vague — rebounds weight the same as points? I'd want to see that before I trust the signal
'Buying raises the price for the next person' — so it's an AMM-style bonding curve? Or a simple order book? That distinction matters for how I'd play it and it's not spelled out
The LeBron $5.59 / $4.20 example: where do those dollar figures come from? Is $100 portfolio money and LeBron costs $4.20 per token, or is that a percentage or index number?
first_impression_elements: The gap framing — 'fair-value price vs. market price, the gap is your edge' — that's the hook, that's what made me stop
The plain-English explainer box ('pretend stock market for NBA players') — unusually honest copy, didn't feel like it was hiding anything
'No signup, no password — it just appears' under step 01, which removed the skepticism reflex immediately
ignored_elements: The '↓Terms explained' link — I'll get to it but it wasn't urgent given the no-real-money framing
The 💬Feedback button — not relevant to me right now
The 'How it works' anchor link — I'd already read the three-step section so it felt redundant

checkpoint.q1_what_am_i_buying: Far as I can tell, it's a fake token tied to a real player — not ownership of anything, just a position in play money that tracks whether the market agrees with the stats or not. It's pretend, which I'm fine with for now.
checkpoint.q2_what_moves_price: Two things from what I read: the stats computer recalculates a fair-value price daily based on real performance, and then other users buying or selling push the market price around. So it's both the player's actual game and the crowd's reaction to it.
checkpoint.q3_hold_week: Honestly the app didn't spell this out clearly. I'd guess the fair-value price keeps updating as LeBron puts up numbers, and the market price drifts with whatever other traders are doing — but whether my position gains or loses without me touching it, I'm not certain. I'd be watching the gap, not just sitting blind.
checkpoint.q4_fair_vs_market: Fair value is what the computer says the player is worth based on his stats. Market price is what people are actually paying right now. The gap between those two is where I live — if the market is sleeping on a guy the stats say is performing, that's my entry point.

first_impression_elements: Bold white-and-orange headline typography — high contrast, immediately readable
The amber/orange CTA button — chunky, impossible to miss, draws the eye fast
Dark grid background — gives it a Bloomberg terminal / trading desk aesthetic
ignored_elements: The 'AWAITING TIP-OFF' status bar at the top — blends into the dark chrome
The 'How it works' secondary link — undersells itself next to that loud button
The '10-second version' explainer block at the bottom — eye never made it there naturally
data.visual_notes: The dark grid and heavy typography signal 'serious trading tool' more than 'fantasy game,' which is a smart visual misdirect — it earns more credibility than the product probably warrants at this stage. The left-heavy layout leaves a lot of dead space on the right half of the viewport, which feels unfinished, like a page that got cut off. Trust ceiling is capped by the absence of any logos, social proof imagery, or data visualizations — it's all text and one button, which reads lean but also reads sparse.
